paper_metadata.title: Understanding Public Acceptability of Climate Policies in Europe
paper_metadata.authors: Shouyu Zhang; Susana Ferreira; Berna Karali
paper_metadata.carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Analyzes support for increasing taxes on fossil fuels such as oil, gas, and coal to reduce climate change, used as the paper's carbon-tax acceptability measure.

determinants.definition: Index of climate-change belief/concern built from skepticism items.
determinants.mechanism: Greater concern about climate change increases willingness to support mitigation taxes.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for increasing taxes on fossil fuels to reduce climate change.
determinants.evidence_quote: Higher trust in the country’s political system and being more concerned about climate change are associated with stronger support for carbon taxation policy.

determinants.definition: Index of trust in parliament, politicians, and political parties.
determinants.mechanism: Greater trust may reduce skepticism about government handling of tax revenues and policy implementation.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for increasing taxes on fossil fuels to reduce climate change.
determinants.evidence_quote: Higher trust in the country’s political system ... [is] associated with stronger support for carbon taxation policy.
determinants.notes: Largest individual-level predictor for carbon tax support.

determinants.definition: Index capturing preference for smaller income differences and fairness.
determinants.mechanism: More egalitarian values may raise support for redistributive or corrective climate taxation.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for increasing taxes on fossil fuels to reduce climate change.
determinants.evidence_quote: Egalitarian attitudes are also associated with higher support for a carbon tax.

determinants.definition: Highest education level of the respondent.
determinants.mechanism: More education may increase understanding of climate policy or willingness to back mitigation taxes.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for increasing taxes on fossil fuels to reduce climate change.
determinants.evidence_quote: Those more educated ... are more supportive of carbon taxation.

determinants.mechanism: Gender differences may reflect higher climate concern or policy support among women.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for increasing taxes on fossil fuels to reduce climate change.
determinants.evidence_quote: Females ... are more supportive of carbon taxation.

determinants.mechanism: Older respondents may be more cost-sensitive or less willing to support new tax burdens.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for increasing taxes on fossil fuels to reduce climate change.
determinants.evidence_quote: Younger respondents ... are more supportive of carbon taxation.

determinants.definition: Self-placement from left to right; higher values indicate more right-wing views.
determinants.mechanism: Right-leaning respondents may oppose government intervention and taxes more strongly.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for increasing taxes on fossil fuels to reduce climate change.
determinants.evidence_quote: ... left-wing political preferences are more supportive of carbon taxation.

determinants.definition: Household income decile, transformed to a continuous income measure.
determinants.mechanism: Higher-income households may feel less burden from the tax and support mitigation more.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for increasing taxes on fossil fuels to reduce climate change.
determinants.evidence_quote: ... those with higher income ... are more supportive of carbon taxation.

determinants.mechanism: Fear of job losses or macroeconomic harm can lower support for climate taxes.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for increasing taxes on fossil fuels to reduce climate change.
determinants.evidence_quote: An additional five percentage points in the perceived unemployment rate would lead to a 0.026-point decrease in carbon taxation support.
determinants.notes: Perceived, not actual, unemployment mattered most.

determinants.definition: Dummy for living in a rural area.
determinants.mechanism: Rural residents may depend more on fossil fuels for transport and equipment, raising expected tax burden.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for increasing taxes on fossil fuels to reduce climate change.
determinants.evidence_quote: Residents of rural areas are less supportive of carbon taxation than urban residents.
determinants.notes: Discussed as likely higher fossil-fuel dependence.

determinants.mechanism: Local air pollution may raise support if tax is seen as improving local environment.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for increasing taxes on fossil fuels to reduce climate change.
determinants.evidence_quote: Exposure to PM2.5 is not statistically significant, suggesting that the local air quality does not alter ... support for carbon taxation.
determinants.notes: Significant for subsidy and efficiency, not carbon tax.

determinants.mechanism: Higher regional economic activity may reduce concerns about the cost of carbon taxation.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for increasing taxes on fossil fuels to reduce climate change.
determinants.evidence_quote: People living in areas with higher GDP ... would be more likely to vote for carbon taxation.

determinants.mechanism: Actual labor-market conditions may shape perceived economic risks of carbon taxation.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for increasing taxes on fossil fuels to reduce climate change.
determinants.evidence_quote: We do not find evidence of a significant association between the actual regional unemployment and people’s support for any climate policy.
determinants.notes: Actual unemployment differed from perceived unemployment.

determinants.mechanism: Higher energy prices may alter perceived costs of climate policies, but no clear carbon-tax effect is found.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for increasing taxes on fossil fuels to reduce climate change.
determinants.evidence_quote: The national electricity price is not statistically significant in explaining the public perception of carbon taxation.
